data.title: Scorpio Tankers Q3 Margin Compression To 32% Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Scorpio Tankers (STNG) has put up another solid quarter in FY 2025, with Q3 revenue of US$241.4 million and basic EPS of US$1.81, supported by net income of US$84.5 million. The company has seen revenue move from US$380.7 million and EPS of US$4.54 in Q2 2024 to US$203.97 million and EPS of US$1.48 in Q4 2024, then to US$214.0 million and EPS of US$1.26 in Q1 2025 and US$230.2 million and EPS of US$1.59 in Q2 2025, giving investors a clear view of how the top line and EPS have tracked into this latest print. With trailing net margin at 32% versus 52.4% a year earlier, the story now turns to how investors weigh solid profitability against a backdrop of moderating margins.

See our full analysis for Scorpio Tankers.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around Scorpio Tankers, and where the data might challenge what investors think they know about the stock.

See what the community is saying about Scorpio Tankers

Advertisement

TTM earnings of US$284.7 million behind past growth story

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to Q3 2025, Scorpio Tankers earned US$284.7 million on US$889.5 million of revenue, compared with single quarter Q3 2025 revenue of US$241.4 million and net income of US$84.5 million.
  • Bulls point to strong five year EPS growth of 38.8% a year, and this latest run of quarterly profits fits that longer history, yet trailing year earnings were negative and:
    • Q3 2025 TTM EPS of US$6.14 is below earlier TTM readings like US$14.60 in Q3 2024. This sits awkwardly with the bullish idea that earnings are on a straight path toward US$473.9 million by 2028.
    • Forecast annual earnings growth of 3.3% a year is also much softer than the broader US market’s 15.7% expectation, so the current profit level alone does not fully back the more upbeat growth assumptions.
Scorpio’s recent profitability gives bulls plenty to talk about, but the step down in TTM EPS is exactly what those bullish projections need to overcome if they are going to play out. 🐂 Scorpio Tankers Bull Case

Margins at 32% vs 52.4% a year ago

  • The trailing net margin sits at 32% compared with 52.4% a year earlier, even though Q3 2025 net income of US$84.5 million on US$241.4 million of revenue still points to a very profitable product tanker operator.
  • Bears argue that shrinking margins and higher future costs could squeeze profitability, and the numbers on hand give that view some support:
    • Forecasts built into the cautious narrative assume margins could fall from 39.2% to 21.7% over three years, while the actual move from 52.4% to 32% over the past year already shows pressure in that direction.
    • Management also highlights that vessel operating expenses are currently helped by recent dry docks, so a future pickup in OpEx would make it harder to reverse the margin compression seen in the trailing figures.
For anyone weighing the cautious take, the margin slide from above 50% to about one third of revenue is a clear data point that lines up with concerns about rising costs and a tougher earnings mix. 🐻 Scorpio Tankers Bear Case

P/E of 11.2x with DCF fair value at US$103.49

  • At a share price of US$67.77, Scorpio Tankers trades on a P/E of 11.2x, below the US Oil & Gas industry at 14.5x and peer average of 12.7x, and also below a DCF fair value estimate of US$103.49 per share.
  • What stands out is how this relatively low multiple and DCF gap intersect with mixed growth expectations:
    • Analysts see revenue drifting lower by 0.4% a year over the next three years on average, yet an 11.2x P/E and the DCF fair value both imply investors are still assigning meaningful value to the company’s earnings base and tanker exposure.
    • The consensus price target of US$76.13 is above the current price, while at the same time forecasts call for earnings growth of only 3.3% a year and an unstable dividend history. This helps explain why the market has not closed the full distance to the DCF estimate.

data.title: There's Reason For Concern Over Sterling Infrastructure, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:STRL) Massive 34% Price Jump
data.description:
Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (NASDAQ:STRL) shareholders have had their patience rewarded with a 34% share price jump in the last month. The annual gain comes to 232% following the latest surge, making investors sit up and take notice.

Following the firm bounce in price, Sterling Infrastructure's price-to-earnings (or "P/E") ratio of 42x might make it look like a strong sell right now compared to the market in the United States, where around half of the companies have P/E ratios below 19x and even P/E's below 11x are quite common. However, the P/E might be quite high for a reason and it requires further investigation to determine if it's justified.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

With earnings growth that's superior to most other companies of late, Sterling Infrastructure has been doing relatively well. It seems that many are expecting the strong earnings performance to persist, which has raised the P/E. If not, then existing shareholders might be a little nervous about the viability of the share price.

Check out our latest analysis for Sterling Infrastructure

Want the full picture on analyst estimates for the company? Then our free report on Sterling Infrastructure will help you uncover what's on the horizon.
Advertisement

Is There Enough Growth For Sterling Infrastructure?

Sterling Infrastructure's P/E ratio would be typical for a company that's expected to deliver very strong growth, and importantly, perform much better than the market.

If we review the last year of earnings growth, the company posted a terrific increase of 73%. The latest three year period has also seen an excellent 256% overall rise in EPS, aided by its short-term performance. Accordingly, shareholders would have probably welcomed those medium-term rates of earnings growth.

Looking ahead now, EPS is anticipated to slump, contracting by 1.7% during the coming year according to the six analysts following the company. Meanwhile, the broader market is forecast to expand by 16%, which paints a poor picture.

With this information, we find it concerning that Sterling Infrastructure is trading at a P/E higher than the market. It seems most investors are hoping for a turnaround in the company's business prospects, but the analyst cohort is not so confident this will happen. Only the boldest would assume these prices are sustainable as these declining earnings are likely to weigh heavily on the share price eventually.

What We Can Learn From Sterling Infrastructure's P/E?

The strong share price surge has got Sterling Infrastructure's P/E rushing to great heights as well. We'd say the price-to-earnings ratio's power isn't primarily as a valuation instrument but rather to gauge current investor sentiment and future expectations.

We've established that Sterling Infrastructure currently trades on a much higher than expected P/E for a company whose earnings are forecast to decline. When we see a poor outlook with earnings heading backwards, we suspect the share price is at risk of declining, sending the high P/E lower. This places shareholders' investments at significant risk and potential investors in danger of paying an excessive premium.

Having said that, be aware Sterling Infrastructure is showing 1 warning sign in our investment analysis, you should know about.

data.title: Barco (ENXTBR:BAR) Margin Improvement Challenges Slowing Earnings Growth Narratives
data.description:
Barco (ENXTBR:BAR) has wrapped up FY 2025 with second half revenue of €509.5 million and basic EPS of €0.58, while the trailing twelve months show revenue of €963.8 million and basic EPS of €0.85, setting a clear benchmark for how the business is currently performing. The company has seen revenue move from €946.6 million to €963.8 million on a trailing basis, with basic EPS shifting from €0.71 to €0.85, giving investors a cleaner read on how profit per share is tracking alongside top line progress. With net profit margins reported at 7.4% over the last year, this earnings release places profitability, quality, and sustainability at the center of the conversation for shareholders.

See our full analysis for Barco.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely held narratives about Barco's growth, risk, and income appeal, and where those stories might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Barco

Advertisement

Margins Point To Better Profit Conversion

  • Net profit margin sits at 7.4% on trailing revenue of €963.8 million and net income of €71.6 million, compared with 6.7% a year earlier, so a slightly larger share of each euro of sales is now turning into profit.
  • Analysts' consensus view links this margin picture to future profit quality, as they expect profit margins to move from 8.0% to 9.1% over the next 3 years, which aligns with the current 7.4% margin but also raises the question of whether regions like EMEA and APAC, where revenue fell 27% and 8%, can support that shift without relying on one time items.
    • The consensus narrative highlights higher margin products and more software in the mix as a key driver, which fits with margins already sitting above the 6.7% level from a year ago.
    • At the same time, critics in that narrative point to ClickShare sales falling 16% and ongoing restructuring costs as potential headwinds that could limit how far margins can move up from the current 7.4%.

Earnings Growth Slows Versus Five Year Pace

  • Earnings grew 13.7% over the last year, compared with 34.5% per year over the past five years, so the recent growth rate is well below the longer term average even though trailing EPS is €0.85 on €71.6 million of net income.
  • What stands out in the bullish narrative is the expectation that operational moves like the Wuxi factory, automation investments and a share buyback program of up to €60 million can keep earnings growing, yet the supplied forecasts of about 11% annual earnings growth and 3.8% annual revenue growth are below the Belgian market averages cited and below the historic 34.5% per year earnings pace.
    • The plan to reach earnings of €99.4 million and EPS of €1.11 by around September 2028 builds on the current base of €71.6 million net income and €0.85 EPS, but assumes Barco can avoid a further step down from the recent 13.7% growth rate.
    • Forecasts also assume shares outstanding fall by 2.43% per year over 3 years, so part of the expected EPS progress comes from a smaller share count rather than repeating the earlier 34.5% per year earnings expansion.

Some investors see this mix of slower recent earnings growth, forecast 11% annual gains, and the ongoing share buyback as a key test for the optimistic case on Barco, and they are watching how consistently management turns operational tweaks into EPS progress. 🐂 Barco Bull Case

Value Case Built On 12x P/E And 5.2% Yield

  • At a share price of €10.58 and a trailing EPS of €0.85, Barco trades on a P/E of roughly 12x, compared with peer and industry averages of about 32.9x and 24.9x, plus a 5.2% dividend yield on top.
  • Bears focus on the fact that revenue is only expected to grow around 3.8% per year and earnings around 11% per year, both below the Belgian market figures cited, and argue that even a DCF fair value of about €19.93 and an analyst consensus price target of €14.42 do not fully offset concerns about weaker regions like EMEA and APAC and the 16% decline in ClickShare, especially if one time benefits such as inventory movements and sale and leaseback gains do not repeat.
    • The current gap between the €10.58 share price and the DCF fair value of €19.93, along with the €14.42 analyst target, is sizeable on paper, yet depends on the company reaching forecast revenue of €1.1b and earnings of €99.4 million.
    • Consensus also assumes a future P/E of 14.2x on those 2028 earnings, slightly above the current 12x, which means part of the perceived value case relies on the market being willing to pay a higher multiple than today despite slower revenue growth than the wider Belgian market.

To own Packaging Corporation of America, you need to be comfortable with a packaging business where pricing power and operational efficiency offset volume softness and margin pressure. data.title: AB InBev (ENXTBR:ABI) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Anheuser-Busch InBev (ENXTBR:ABI) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of about US$15.6b and basic EPS of US$0.99, rounding out a year where trailing 12 month revenue reached roughly US$59.3b and EPS came in at US$3.45. Over the past few quarters, revenue has moved from US$14.8b in Q4 2024 to US$15.0b in Q2 2025 and US$15.1b in Q3 2025, while quarterly EPS has ranged between US$0.53 and US$1.08, setting up a picture of higher trailing profitability alongside a net profit margin that has moved from 9.8% to 11.5% over the last year. For investors, this earnings season update puts margins and bottom line efficiency firmly in focus as they weigh the balance of risks and rewards.

See our full analysis for Anheuser-Busch InBev.

With the latest results on the table, the next step is to see how these revenue and EPS trends line up with the widely held narratives around Anheuser-Busch InBev, and where the numbers might be telling a different story.

TTM profit growth outpaces revenue

  • Over the last 12 months, net income reached about US$6.8b on revenue of roughly US$59.3b, so profit grew 16.8% while revenue growth was cited at 4.7% per year.
  • Bulls point to premium and Beyond Beer brands as future drivers, and this stronger profit growth already lines up with that idea. It is worth noting that recent quarterly revenue went from US$15.0b in Q2 2025 to US$15.6b in Q4, while quarterly EPS moved around between US$0.53 and US$1.08, which shows earnings are still quite sensitive to seasonality and mix.
    • The bullish view talks about revenue growing 6.6% per year and margins moving from about 10.4% to 15.4%. The actual trailing margin sits at 11.5% after moving up from 9.8%, so the direction matches but the jump analysts are using is larger than what is in the current figures.
    • Bulls also assume earnings can reach US$10.9b and EPS of US$6.3 by around 2028, compared with trailing net income of about US$6.8b and EPS of US$3.45. This means a lot of the bullish upside rests on earnings almost doubling from where they are today.

Supporters who think this profit momentum can keep compounding may want to see how that powers the upbeat narrative for ABI in more detail. 🐂 Anheuser-Busch InBev Bull Case

Margins and debt sit at the center

The margin figures give both support and a check on that view, because while margin has moved up into the low double digits, revenue growth of 4.7% per year is below the 7.5% market benchmark, so a lot of the recent improvement is coming from profitability rather than faster top line expansion.

If you are weighing those debt concerns against the margin picture, it helps to see how skeptics connect the dots across different regions and products. 🐻 Anheuser-Busch InBev Bear Case

data.title: Is Heineken (ENXTAM:HEIA) Pricing Look Interesting After Recent Share Price Strength
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Heineken at around €78 a share still offers value, the key question is how its current price compares with what the business might reasonably be worth.
  • The stock has recently been firm, with returns of 6.0% over the last 7 days, 14.4% over the last 30 days, 13.2% year to date, and 0.8% over the last year. However, the 3 year and 5 year returns of negative 12.9% and negative 1.0% show a much flatter longer term picture.
  • Recent news around Heineken has focused on its position as a major global brewer and how it is managing brand strength and operations in different markets. This helps frame how investors think about its long term prospects. These themes often influence how the market prices the shares, particularly when sentiment shifts between focusing on shorter term share price moves and longer term fundamentals.
  • Right now, Heineken scores 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks, giving it a valuation score of 3. Next we will look at what different valuation methods indicate about that number, before finishing with a way of thinking about value that can give you a fuller picture than any single model.

Find out why Heineken's 0.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Heineken Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future euros are worth in today’s money.

For Heineken, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €2.57b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2028, with Simply Wall St then extrapolating free cash flow out to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow for 2026 is €2.66b and for 2035 is €3.39b, with intermediate years gradually stepping up between those figures.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €150.96 per share. Compared with a recent share price around €78, this output suggests the stock is 48.3% undervalued according to this DCF model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Heineken is undervalued by 48.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 234 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Heineken.

Approach 2: Heineken Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Heineken, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they feel those earnings are.

Heineken currently trades on a P/E of 23.05x. That sits above the Beverage industry average of 17.72x, but slightly below the peer group average of 24.82x. On their own, these comparisons show where the market is pricing Heineken relative to its sector and closest competitors, but they do not fully reflect the company’s specific growth outlook, profitability or risk profile.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio tackles that by estimating what a company’s P/E might be given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and identified risks. For Heineken, this Fair Ratio is 21.36x, which is below the current 23.05x P/E. On this basis, the shares screens as somewhat expensive relative to what the Fair Ratio model suggests would be a more neutral level.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 100 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Heineken Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply the stories investors tell about a company that link its business outlook to a set of numbers like future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives to set your own view on Heineken, compare it with others, and see in real time how each view changes when new earnings or news arrive. For example, one Heineken Narrative might lean closer to a fair value around €64.62 with more cautious assumptions, while another leans toward €110.19 with more optimistic expectations. By comparing each Narrative’s Fair Value with today’s share price, you get a clear, easy to use framework for assessing whether the stock looks expensive, cheap or roughly in line with your own story.

For Heineken however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Heineken Narratives:

🐂 Heineken Bull Case

Fair value in this bull case: €85.16 per share

Implied discount to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 8.3% below the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 3.73% a year

  • Focuses on premium brands, emerging markets and low or no alcohol products as key drivers of revenue and margin strength.
  • Assumes gradual improvement in profit margins and steady earnings growth, supported by cost savings and digital efficiency programs.
  • Flags currency swings, reliance on emerging markets and pressure in mature European markets as the main things that could upset this story.

🐻 Heineken Bear Case

Fair value in this bear case: €77.50 per share

Implied premium to this fair value at a €78.08 share price: about 0.7% above the narrative fair value

Revenue growth assumption used in this view: 6.62% a year

  • Builds a valuation mainly from a DCF model using free cash flow projections over 5 years and two different terminal value methods.
  • Arrives at an intrinsic value range of €72 to €91 per share and then applies a 15% margin of safety, which pulls the working fair value down to about €77.50.
  • Highlights that at a share price just above €78, Heineken sits close to this cautious fair value, so any change in cash flow, discount rate or growth assumptions can quickly shift the margin of safety up or down.

data.title: Raute Oyj (HLSE:RAUTE) Margin Resilience Challenges Cautious Earnings Narratives
data.description:

Over the past few quarters, the company has seen revenue move from €51.9 million and EPS of €0.70 in Q1 2025 to €43.8 million with EPS of €0.20 in Q2, then €43.7 million with EPS of €0.66 in Q3 before landing at the latest Q4 figures, giving investors a full view of how the top and bottom line have tracked through the year. Margins Holding Up Around 6.3%

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Raute generated €12.9 million of net income on €175.7 million of revenue, which works out to a 6.3% net margin compared with 5.1% in the prior period mentioned in the inputs.
  • Analysts' consensus view expects margins to move higher even if revenue softens, which creates an interesting tension:
    • Forecasts in the inputs point to revenue declining about 1.4% per year over the next three years, yet margins are assumed to rise from 6.3% to 7.4%, so the story leans on Raute keeping more profit from each euro of sales.
    • Q4 2025 net income of €3.6 million on €36.2 million of revenue sits roughly in line with that 6.3% trailing margin, which supports the idea that recent profitability levels are already close to the margin profile analysts are building into their models.

Earnings Growth Versus Softer Revenue Trend

  • Over the last year in the dataset, earnings grew 25.3% while the trailing revenue line moved from €204.6 million at 2024 Q4 to €175.7 million at 2025 Q4, so profit growth has come alongside a softer top line in the recent figures provided.
  • Consensus narrative inputs highlight a more cautious path for both revenue and earnings, and the current numbers cut both ways:
    • On one hand, the company has a five year earnings growth rate of 57.6% per year and trailing EPS of €2.12, which supports the view that Raute can still generate healthy profit even when revenue is not expanding quickly.
    • On the other hand, forecasts in the data call for roughly 0.3% per year earnings decline and 1.4% per year revenue decline over the next three years, so the recent 25.3% earnings lift offers a higher bar that future results would need to match for this stronger earnings pattern to continue.
On a year where profit growth outpaced the top line, some investors are asking whether that pattern can last, or if the forecasted dip in revenue and earnings will start to bite into Raute's story, which is exactly what bulls and bears debate in more depth in the dedicated Raute narratives. 🐂 Raute Oyj Bull Case

Low P/E And DCF Gap To €23.65

    • The data flags that the 3.89% dividend yield is not well covered by free cash flow, so skeptics question how sustainable that payout is if revenue declines about 1.4% per year and earnings about 0.3% per year as forecasts suggest.

Verisure (OM:VSURE) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €964.7 million and a basic EPS loss of €0.12, while trailing 12 month figures show revenue of about €3.7 billion and a basic EPS loss of €0.30. €3.7b revenue with €255.9m loss still points to margin pressure

  • Over the last 12 months Verisure generated about €3.7b in revenue and reported a net loss of €255.9m, which means the business is bringing in a lot of sales but costs are still high enough to keep it firmly in loss making territory.

  • Cautious investors highlight this mix of a high 62.9x P/E and a relatively low 2.4% net margin as a key risk, and those concerns sit directly against forecasts calling for earnings to grow about 51.7% per year over the next three years from a trailing base of SEK55.5m.
    • The large gap between the current price of SEK24.60 and the SEK98.94 DCF fair value suggests one view of considerable upside, yet the premium P/E and slim margins give plenty of room for questions about how quickly that gap might narrow.
    • Short term share price volatility that has been higher than the Swedish market adds another layer for bears, since a rich multiple combined with swings in the price can make any disappointment in the 51.7% earnings growth path feel more painful.
